claim: Coding is the first breakout use case for LLMs with clear product-market fit: customers are pulling it out of vendors' hands
actionable: If building AI products, prioritize coding automation as your beachhead before attempting broader domain applications where product fit remains uncertain.
source_title: The Economics of AI Usage and What's Next For SaaS

claim: Foundation models themselves are not a differentiated product; the value will move up the stack to applications that solve specific problems
actionable: If evaluating LLM companies, assess their ability to build application layer solutions (vertical software, enterprise tools) rather than betting on model differentiation alone.
source_title: The Economics of AI Usage and What's Next For SaaS

claim: Models are trending toward commodity infrastructure with pricing pressure: just as mobile networks built $1T infrastructure but captured no value while apps and platforms did, foundation models may face similar dynamics
actionable: When predicting enterprise AI adoption, apply the infrastructure commodification model: expect downward pricing pressure as multiple competing vendors (Google, OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.) reach feature parity.
source_title: The Economics of AI Usage and What's Next For SaaS

claim: Pricing for AI inference is in severe disequilibrium; early adopters see massive price swings ($10k+ bills for token overages) that will eventually stabilize as supply grows and models become interchangeable
actionable: When budgeting for AI token costs, assume current per-token pricing will drop significantly as models commoditize; build cost controls and monitor usage to avoid surprise bills.
source_title: The Economics of AI Usage and What's Next For SaaS

claim: Major cloud providers (Google, Meta, Microsoft) face an existential FOMO trap: they must continue massive capex investment in AI even if ROI is uncertain, because ceding ground would be catastrophic to their core business
actionable: Don't expect major AI capex to slow significantly in the next 2-3 years based on ROI pressures alone; competitive dynamics will likely push it higher.
source_title: The Economics of AI Usage and What's Next For SaaS

claim: It's hard to measure AI ROI early because most documented benefits are vague (better analytics, faster support) rather than clear revenue increases; this may persist for years as with cloud
actionable: When making AI investment decisions, set expectations that clear unit economics may take 2-3 years to emerge; rely on leading indicators (productivity, velocity) rather than waiting for bottom-line profit impact.
source_title: The Economics of AI Usage and What's Next For SaaS

claim: AI/LLM use inside enterprises may be driven more by competitive necessity (everyone else adopted it) than real ROI, capturing productivity gains as consumer surplus rather than revenue
actionable: When evaluating enterprise AI adoption metrics, separate aspirational ROI claims from actual competitive pressure; assume some usage is defensive not offensive.
source_title: The Economics of AI Usage and What's Next For SaaS

claim: A chatbot UI is a limited v1 that works for only some tasks; real value requires custom tooling, data setup, configuration, control, and domain-specific UX design
actionable: Don't assume off-the-shelf chatbot interfaces will drive adoption; plan for custom UX, domain-specific workflows, and integration with existing data/tools in your vertical.
source_title: The Economics of AI Usage and What's Next For SaaS

claim: Enterprise software decisions don't standardize on a model choice (e.g., Claude vs OpenAI); rather, model selection is abstracted away from buying decisions just like cloud provider is abstracted in SaaS
actionable: Build your AI application to be agnostic to the underlying model provider; assume customers won't choose your product based on which LLM you use.
source_title: The Economics of AI Usage and What's Next For SaaS

claim: The three levers that unlock new use cases are: price elasticity (do cheaper, do more of the same), removing barriers to entry (expensive capital requirements go away), and unlocking previously impossible scenarios (like Spotify's all-music for $15/month)
actionable: When exploring new AI use cases, ask: (1) does it make existing workflows cheaper? (2) does it remove a capital barrier? (3) does it enable something that was cost-prohibitive? If none apply, the use case may not materialize.
source_title: The Economics of AI Usage and What's Next For SaaS

claim: With LLMs applied to advertising/e-commerce, systems can now understand *what* products are and *why* people buy them (not just correlation), enabling far better recommendations and ad targeting than Google/Meta today
actionable: If building e-commerce or ad-tech products, invest in LLM-powered product understanding and buyer intent modeling as a core differentiation vs. legacy correlation-based systems.
source_title: The Economics of AI Usage and What's Next For SaaS

claim: The key question for large enterprises isn't 'what can AI do?' but 'what is the job we're actually trying to do and what are the hidden, undocumented processes that drive outcomes?' — questions best answered by consultants, not technologists
actionable: When deploying AI in law/finance/consulting, partner with domain experts to uncover implicit workflows before attempting automation; generic AI won't find what stakeholders can't articulate.
source_title: The Economics of AI Usage and What's Next For SaaS

claim: Unlike previous platform shifts (mobile, cloud, web), AI's impact is unpredictable at the physical constraint level: models could suddenly get 100x cheaper, 2x better, or hit diminishing returns—none of these futures is knowable now
actionable: Don't anchor long-term AI strategy to current model pricing or performance; assume at least one major discontinuity (breakthrough or plateau) will occur within 3 years.
source_title: The Economics of AI Usage and What's Next For SaaS

claim: Large capex deployments ($700B+ annually from major tech firms) are financially unsustainable long-term; there's a hard cap (countries don't spend >$10T yearly on all infrastructure), forcing eventual slowdown
actionable: When projecting AI infrastructure spend, assume capex will plateau within 3-5 years due to absolute financial limits, not ROI optimization.
source_title: The Economics of AI Usage and What's Next For SaaS
